old_value: {"id":"Demarch-llc","title":"[interfere] Update model tier routing to MoE-first models","description":"Replace the current complexity-to-model mapping in routing.yaml and AGENTS.md with newer MoE models that are significantly faster and better quality. MoE models give big-model quality at small-model speed because only a fraction of params are active per token. Also update the memory budget in AGENTS.md to reflect actual model sizes.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:15Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:15Z"}

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-5pa","title":"[interfere] Investigate flash-moe integration for 397B MoE inference","description":"Research integrating flash-moe (github.com/danveloper/flash-moe) into the interfere pipeline. flash-moe is a pure C/Metal engine that runs Qwen3.5-397B-A17B by streaming expert weights from SSD. On a 48GB M3 Max it achieves 4.36 tok/s at 4-bit. On our 128GB M5 Max, expert caching would be dramatically better (most active experts stay in RAM), potentially 8-12+ tok/s. Integration options: (1) subprocess bridge calling flash-moe binary, (2) port SSD-streaming techniques into our MLX pipeline, (3) hybrid where flash-moe handles C3+ tasks and mlx-lm handles C1-C2. This would be a game-changer for sovereignty — running a 397B model locally. Should be an interlab experiment campaign.","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:19Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T15:52:19Z"}

new_value: Feasibility study complete. M5 Max SSD benchmarks: 36 GB/s seq, 27 GB/s random pread (1.5-2x faster than M3 Max test rig). Estimated 7-10 tok/s for 397B model at 4-bit. Recommend subprocess bridge integration. Doc: docs/investigations/2026-03-26-flash-moe-feasibility.md

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qdg","title":"[interfere] Experiment: confidence cascade with multi-model waterfall","description":"Implement the confidence cascade described in routing.yaml: start inference with the smallest model, measure first-3-token average probability. If confidence \u003e 0.8: accept. If 0.6-0.8: try next larger model. If \u003c 0.6: escalate to cloud. This is the quality gate for local-vs-cloud routing — the key mechanism that makes sovereignty practical. Requires: (1) token-level probability extraction from mlx-lm, (2) fast model switching in the Metal worker, (3) latency budget tracking (cascade can't be slower than just calling the big model).","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:23:45Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:23:45Z"}

Expected: 40-60% TTFT reduction for second and subsequent agent requests in a sprint.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:24:15Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:24:15Z"}

{"status":"in_progress"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-dw8","title":"[interfere] Experiment: quantized KV cache (Q8K/Q4V)","description":"Quantize the Key-Value cache to reduce memory pressure. Keys to 8-bit, Values to 4-bit (asymmetric quantization since values are more tolerant). KIVI (2024) shows 2-bit KV is feasible. ThinK (2024) shows channel pruning in KV cache gives 20% reduction composable with quantization. On 128GB M5 Max this could: (1) fit larger KV context windows, (2) leave more RAM for page cache when SSD-streaming experts, (3) enable running 397B with larger batch sizes. The AGENTS.md already references Q8K/Q4V in the memory budget.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## KV Cache Quantization Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-35B-A3B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs (warm cache)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 85.8 | 80.3 | 43.1/110.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=8 | 86.0 | 81.7 | 44.1/111.4 | 0.155s |
| kv_bits=4 | 81.6 | 75.8 | 35.8/103.9 | 0.171s |
| kv_bits=2 | 79.7 | 78.3 | 42.1/106.4 | 0.160s |

### 500-token runs (thermal throttled)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 53.8 | 67.1 | 27.5/122.5 | 0.218s |
| kv_bits=8 | 57.9 | 65.4 | 26.1/112.7 | 0.211s |
| kv_bits=4 | 53.3 | 59.0 | 25.1/110.1 | 0.247s |
| kv_bits=2 | 49.8 | 54.7 | 25.9/98.9 | 0.236s |

### Key findings:
1. **Quality: zero degradation at all levels** — factual, reasoning, and instruction outputs identical through kv_bits=2
2. **kv_bits=8: free lunch** — no throughput penalty, 2x KV memory reduction
3. **kv_bits=4: ~5% throughput cost** — 4x KV memory reduction, worth it for larger models
4. **kv_bits=2: ~7% throughput cost** — 8x KV memory reduction, quality still holds at 500 tokens
5. Thermal throttling dominates at 500-token runs (heavy thermal state), making speed differences noisy
6. The real win is memory headroom for 122B/397B models, not speed on the 35B

### Recommendation: default to kv_bits=8 for all models, use kv_bits=4 for 122B+"}

Keys to 8-bit, Values to 4-bit (asymmetric quantization since values are more tolerant). KIVI (2024) shows 2-bit KV is feasible. ThinK (2024) shows channel pruning in KV cache gives 20% reduction composable with quantization. On 128GB M5 Max this could: (1) fit larger KV context windows, (2) leave more RAM for page cache when SSD-streaming experts, (3) enable running 397B with larger batch sizes. The AGENTS.md already references Q8K/Q4V in the memory budget.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:56Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T17:44:12Z"}

Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts. 122B total, 10B active, ~65GB, fits in RAM on 128GB M5 Max.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## 122B Benchmark Results (Qwen3.5-122B-A10B-4bit, M5 Max 128GB)

### 100-token runs
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | P5/P95 | TTFT |
|--------|-------------|------|--------|------|
| none (fp16) | 49.9 | 45.7 | 26.0/59.0 | 0.225s |
| kv_bits=8 | 46.0 | 44.0 | 25.8/56.7 | 0.240s |
| kv_bits=4 | 33.0 | 30.7 | 18.5/37.7 | 0.341s |

### 500-token run (baseline only)
| Median tok/s | Mean | TTFT | Thermal |
|-------------|------|------|---------|
| 24.6 | 26.4 | 0.492s | heavy |

### Comparison: 35B vs 122B
| Metric | 35B (3B active) | 122B (10B active) |
|--------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Median tok/s (100t) | 85.8 | 49.9 |
| TTFT | 0.155s | 0.225s |
| RAM footprint | ~19GB | ~65GB |
| Quality | Good | Better (cleaner reasoning) |

### Key findings:
1. **50 tok/s is usable** for real-time generation — comparable to cloud latency
2. kv_bits=8 has slight penalty on 122B (~8%) vs free lunch on 35B — larger model = more KV computation
3. kv_bits=4 has significant penalty on 122B (~34%) — not recommended at short context
4. 500-token runs hit thermal throttling hard (nominal→heavy, 50→25 tok/s)
5. 122B quality visibly better than 35B on reasoning tasks"}

Measure: tok/s, time-to-first-token, quality on standard prompts. 122B total, 10B active, ~65GB, fits in RAM on 128GB M5 Max.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:15:11Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T18:20:46Z"}

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-bu3","title":"[interfere] Experiment: speculative decoding with small draft model","description":"Use Qwen3.5-9B as a draft model to accelerate generation from larger models (122B, 397B). Speculative decoding generates candidate tokens with the fast model, then verifies in parallel with the target model. Expected: 2-2.5x speedup (per Leviathan et al. 2023). mlx-lm had a speculative_decoding example. Measure: tok/s improvement, rejection rate, quality parity.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Speculative Decoding Results: 35B draft → 122B target

### Results (M5 Max 128GB)
| Config | Median tok/s | Mean | TTFT | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|------|------|-------------|
| 122B alone | 49.9 | 45.7 | 0.225s | — |
| +35B draft (3 tok/step) | 14.9 | 14.9 | 0.688s | **3.3x slower** |
| +35B draft (5 tok/step) | 11.4 | 11.4 | 0.909s | **4.4x slower** |

### Why it failed:
1. **Draft model too large**: 35B (19GB) is not small enough — ideal draft is 10-20x smaller
2. **Memory bandwidth saturated**: 84GB total model weight leaves minimal headroom
3. **MoE active ratio too close**: 3B active (draft) vs 10B active (target) — only 3.3x gap
4. **Thermal throttling**: heavy state throughout, both models competing for bandwidth

### What would work:
- Need a ~1-3B draft model with matching Qwen3.5 tokenizer
- Qwen3.5-9B-4bit (~5GB) would be ideal if available on mlx-community
- Alternative: self-speculative decoding (LayerSkip, Demarch-qbv) avoids separate draft entirely"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-bu3","title":"[interfere] Experiment: speculative decoding with small draft model","description":"Use Qwen3.5-9B as a draft model to accelerate generation from larger models (122B, 397B). Speculative decoding generates candidate tokens with the fast model, then verifies in parallel with the target model. Expected: 2-2.5x speedup (per Leviathan et al. 2023). mlx-lm had a speculative_decoding example. Measure: tok/s improvement, rejection rate, quality parity.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:36Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T19:14:32Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Speculative Decoding — Full Results (M5 Max 128GB)

### 0.8B draft → 35B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 35B alone | 85.8 | — |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 76.0 | **-11%** |

### 0.8B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| Config | Median tok/s | vs Baseline |
|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 122B alone | 49.9 | — |
| +0.8B draft (2 tok/step) | 36.4 | -27% (thermal confound) |
| +0.8B draft (3 tok/step) | 46.9 | **-6%** |
| +0.8B draft (5 tok/step) | 40.8 | -18% |

### 35B draft → 122B target (100 tokens)
| +35B draft (3 tok/step) | 14.9 | **-70%** |
| +35B draft (5 tok/step) | 11.4 | -77% |

### Key findings:
1. **Speculative decoding does not help on M5 Max** — all configurations slower than baseline
2. **Root cause: memory bandwidth bottleneck** — Apple Silicon unified memory means spec dec trades compute savings (which we don't need) for extra memory traffic (which is our bottleneck)
3. **Dense→MoE mismatch hurts acceptance rate** — 0.8B dense draft generates different tokens from MoE target
4. **Larger draft = more waste** — 35B draft is catastrophically slow
5. **Optimal if using at all**: 0.8B draft with 3 tokens/step (only -6% on 122B)
6. **Self-speculative (LayerSkip) is the right path** for Apple Silicon — no extra model, no extra memory traffic"}

old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qbv","title":"[interfere] Experiment: LayerSkip / self-speculative early exit","description":"Implement early exit with self-speculative verification (LayerSkip, arXiv 2404.16710). Early layers generate draft tokens, remaining layers verify. No separate draft model needed — uses the target model's own early layers. Expected: 1.8-2.2x speedup. This extends our existing early_exit.py hook from entropy-based exit to self-speculative verification. Key advantage: shares compute/activations between draft and verify, reducing memory vs standard speculative decoding.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Confidence Profile Results (Early Exit Calibration)

### Tokens above confidence thresholds
| Model | >80% | >90% | >95% | >99% |
|-------|------|------|------|------|
| 0.8B dense | 71.4% | 64.2% | 56.8% | 44.8% |
| 35B MoE (3B active) | 81.7% | 76.1% | 71.0% | 64.4% |
| 122B MoE (10B active) | 84.1% | 78.7% | 74.0% | 67.5% |

### Key findings:
1. **Larger models are MORE confident** — higher fraction of easy tokens
2. **67.5% of 122B tokens have >99% confidence** — huge LayerSkip potential
3. **Creative writing has lowest confidence** (41-60% >99%) — layer skipping should be conservative here
4. **Coding and instruction following are most confident** (67-84% >99%) — best candidates for early exit
5. **MoE architecture concentrates confidence** — expert routing already "simplifies" many tokens

### Theoretical speedup if LayerSkip could skip 50% of layers for >99% tokens:
- 122B: 67.5% × 0.5 = 33.75% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (50→75 tok/s)
- 35B: 64.4% × 0.5 = 32.2% compute saved → ~1.5x speedup (86→129 tok/s)"}
old_value: {"id":"Demarch-qbv","title":"[interfere] Experiment: LayerSkip / self-speculative early exit","description":"Implement early exit with self-speculative verification (LayerSkip, arXiv 2404.16710). Early layers generate draft tokens, remaining layers verify. No separate draft model needed — uses the target model's own early layers. Expected: 1.8-2.2x speedup. This extends our existing early_exit.py hook from entropy-based exit to self-speculative verification. Key advantage: shares compute/activations between draft and verify, reducing memory vs standard speculative decoding.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"mistakeknot@vibeguider.org","created_at":"2026-03-26T16:22:37Z","created_by":"mistakeknot","updated_at":"2026-03-26T19:29:25Z"}

new_value:
{"notes":"## Interlab Campaign Assessment

Current interfere experiments are measurement/characterization, not optimization — autoresearch shines at iterative code changes with measurable metrics.

### kv_group_size sweep (manual):
- {32, 64, 128} all within noise (85.8-91.9 tok/s on 35B with kv_bits=8)
- MLX only supports {32, 64, 128}. Not worth autoresearch.

### When to launch autoresearch:
1. After LayerSkip implementation — tune exit threshold, layer skip pattern
2. After TurboQuant kernel — tune PolarQuant grid parameters
3. Prompt template optimization — tune chat template for MoE expert routing"}
